title: 10 best herbal teas for mucus in stomach

remedies.remedy_desc: Licorice tea helps with mucus in the stomach because it contains substances called flavonoids and triterpenoids, which have anti-inflammatory and mucilage-like properties. These compounds form a protective coating on the mucous membrane of the stomach, reducing inflammation and irritation. Moreover, licorice tea may help increase the mucus secretion in the gastrointestinal tract, which can provide a soothing effect and help to eliminate excess mucus. Additionally, licorice tea has been found to have antimicrobial properties, which might help to eradicate harmful bacteria contributing to the production of mucus in the stomach. Lastly, licorice tea's demulcent effect can help to alleviate symptoms of stomach discomfort, such as heartburn, acid reflux, and bloating, often associated with the presence of mucus in the stomach.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice plant are commonly used in medicinal teas for mucus in the stomach due to their anti-inflammatory properties, which can help soothe and heal the gastrointestinal tract.
Rhyzomes: The rhizomes of the licorice plant contain glycyrrhizin, a compound with expectorant and soothing effects, making it useful for breaking up and expelling mucus from the stomach.
Leaves: The leaves of the licorice plant are used in teas to treat mucus in the stomach, as they contain flavonoids that have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties.
Flowers: The flowers of the licorice plant are used in teas to treat mucus in the stomach due to their high content of antioxidants, which can help reduce inflammation and support the immune system.
Seeds: The seeds of the licorice plant are used in teas to treat mucus in the stomach due to their natural expectorant properties, which can help break up and expel mucus.

Bark: The bark of the licorice plant is used in teas to treat mucus in the stomach due to its high concentration of triterpenoid saponins, which have been shown to have expectorant and anti-inflammatory effects.
remedy_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: This compound is the primary active component in licorice, and has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties, helping to reduce irritation and clear mucus from the stomach.
Flavonoids: These antioxidant compounds found in licorice have been shown to help reduce inflammation and irritation in the stomach, and may help soothe mucous membranes.
Saponins: These compounds found in licorice have been shown to have expectorant and soothing properties, helping to break up and remove mucus from the stomach.
Glycyrrhetic acid: This compound, a metabolite of glycyrrhizin, has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and mucosal protective effects, helping to reduce irritation and soothe the stomach lining.
remedy_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water and add 1 teaspoon of dried licorice root.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to coat your stomach and loosen mucus.

remedies.remedy_desc: Marshmallow tea helps with mucus in the stomach because it contains mucilage, a substance that becomes a gel when mixed with water, which can provide a protective barrier to the stomach lining, soothe irritation, and reduce inflammation. This soothing effect can help to reduce the production of excess mucus in the stomach, providing relief from discomfort. Additionally, the anti-inflammatory properties of marshmallow tea may further help to reduce inflammation in the stomach, which can contribute to the reduction of mucus. Marshmallow tea may also help to stimulate the production of digestive juices, which can improve digestion and reduce the likelihood of stomach discomfort and mucus production. Furthermore, marshmallow tea is a natural alternative to over-the-counter medications for stomach discomfort, and may be a safer option for those who are sensitive to medication or who prefer a more natural approach.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The marshmallow leaves are rich in mucilage, which can help soothe and protect the stomach lining, providing relief from mucus and inflammation.
Flowers: Marshmallow flowers contain flavonoids and polysaccharides that have anti-inflammatory and mucilaginous properties, making them useful for treating mucus-related stomach issues.
Roots: The roots of marshmallow plants have a high mucilage content, providing a demulcent effect that coats the stomach lining, helping to reduce irritation and soothe mucus membranes.
Seeds: Marshmallow seeds are a source of mucilage and starch, which can help alleviate symptoms of mucus in the stomach by creating a protective layer over the mucous membrane.
remedy_constituents: Mucilage: The mucilage in marshmallow coats the stomach lining, providing a protective barrier that can help soothe and reduce irritation, including mucus in the stomach.
Polysaccharides: The polysaccharides in marshmallow have demulcent properties, meaning they can help to soothe and heal mucous membranes, reducing inflammation and irritation in the stomach.
Flavonoids: Marshmallow flavonoids, such as luteolin and quercetin, have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the stomach and alleviate mucus production.
Pectin: The pectin in marshmallow has soothing properties that can help protect the stomach lining, reducing irritation and mucus buildup.

Ascorbic acid: Marshmallow contains ascorbic acid, which has antioxidant properties that can help reduce oxidative stress and inflammation in the stomach, potentially helping to alleviate mucus production.
remedy_recipe: In a saucepan, combine 2 cups of water, 1 tablespoon of crushed dried marshmallow root, and bring to a simmer.
Reduce heat to low, cover, and let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Add honey or sugar to taste, stirring until fully dissolved.
Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day for mucus relief in the stomach.

remedies.remedy_desc: Slippery elm tea helps with mucus in the stomach because it contains mucilage, a substance that becomes a slick gel when mixed with water. This gel can provide a protective barrier to the stomach lining, soothing irritation and inflammation. Furthermore, it helps to neutralize stomach acid, reducing the amount of stomach irritation. As a result, it can help to alleviate symptoms associated with excess mucus in the stomach, such as nausea, acid reflux, and stomach pain. Slippery elm tea is also believed to have antibacterial properties, which may help to eliminate the bacteria responsible for the production of excess mucus in the stomach. Ultimately, slippery elm tea can help to reduce inflammation, neutralize stomach acid, and eliminate harmful bacteria, leading to a reduction in mucus production and improved digestive health.
remedy_parts: Bark: The bark of the slippery elm plant is commonly used to make medicinal tea for mucus in the stomach due to its high mucilage content, which can help soothe and protect the stomach lining.

Inner Bark: The inner bark of the slippery elm tree is used to make teas for treating mucus in the stomach because of its demulcent properties, which can help reduce inflammation and irritation in the digestive tract.
Leaves: Slippery elm leaves are used in teas for relieving mucus in the stomach due to their high antioxidant content, which can help protect the stomach lining and reduce inflammation.
Seeds: Slippery elm seeds are used in teas to soothe mucus in the stomach because they contain mucilage, which can help form a protective coating in the digestive tract and reduce irritation.

Stems: The stems of the slippery elm plant are used to make teas that can help alleviate mucus in the stomach due to their mucilaginous properties, which can help soothe and lubricate the digestive tract.
remedy_constituents: Mucilage: Slippery elm's high mucilage content forms a soothing film over the mucous membranes, providing relief from irritation and excess mucus in the stomach.
Tannins: The astringent tannins in slippery elm help reduce inflammation, controlling the production of excess mucus and supporting stomach health.
Starch: Slippery elm's starch content acts as a demulcent, protecting the stomach lining and helping to alleviate irritation and mucus buildup.
Gallic acid: This antioxidant compound found in slippery elm helps decrease inflammation in the stomach, reducing mucus secretion and promoting gastrointestinal health.
remedy_recipe: "In a pot, boil 2 cups of water for 5 minutes."
"Stir in 1-2 teaspoons of dried slippery elm bark, then reduce heat and simmer for 10 minutes."
"Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to coat your stomach and throat."

remedies.remedy_desc: Thyme tea helps with mucus in the stomach because it has antimicrobial and antispasmodic properties that can help soothe and reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, potentially lessening the production of mucus. Additionally, thyme contains compounds called flavonoids, which have been shown to have expectorant effects, helping to break up and expel mucus from the body. Thyme tea may also stimulate the production of gastric juices, aiding in digestion and the breakdown of food, which can help prevent the formation of excess mucus in the stomach. Furthermore, thyme has been used traditionally as a medicine to treat coughs and respiratory ailments, suggesting that its mucus-reducing properties may extend beyond the digestive system. However, it's important to note that more research is needed to fully understand the mechanisms by which thyme tea may help with mucus in the stomach.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Thyme leaves are commonly used in medicinal teas for mucus in the stomach due to their natural antispasmodic and expectorant properties.
Flowers: The flowers of the thyme plant are used in tea to soothe stomach discomfort and reduce mucus production, thanks to their calming and anti-inflammatory effects.
Stems: Thyme stems contain essential oils and compounds that help break down mucus and provide relief from congestion in the stomach.
Seeds: Thyme seeds are rich in antiseptic properties, making them a valuable addition to medicinal teas for mucus in the stomach, as they can help eliminate harmful bacteria.
remedy_constituents: Thymol: Thymol, a major component of thyme, has antimicrobial and expectorant properties, helping to break up mucus and clear respiratory passages, including the stomach.
Carvacrol: Carvacrol, another key compound in thyme, has been shown to have antispasmodic effects, which can help alleviate mucus-related coughing and discomfort in the stomach.

Flavonoids: Thyme flavonoids, such as apigenin and luteolin, have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, which can help reduce irritation and inflammation in the stomach, often associated with mucus production.
Volatile oils: The volatile oils in thyme, which include thymol, carvacrol, and others, can help stimulate mucus production in the stomach, facilitating the elimination of irritants and pathogens.
Tannins: Tannins present in thyme have astringent properties, which can help protect the mucous membrane in the stomach, reducing excessive mucus secretion caused by inflammation or irritation.
Terpenes: Terpenes, like thymol and carvacrol, in thyme, have been found to have mucolytic properties, breaking down mucus and making it easier to expel from the stomach.
remedy_recipe: In a small pot, bring 1 cup of water to a boil, then add 1 teaspoon of dried thyme leaves.
Let the mixture simmer for 5-10 minutes to allow the thyme to release its medicinal properties.
Strain the tea to remove the thyme leaves, and discard the used leaves.
Drink the tea while it is still warm, up to three times a day, to help soothe a stomach with mucus.

remedies.remedy_desc: Anise tea helps with mucus in the stomach because it contains anethole, a compound with antimicrobial and expectorant properties that help eliminate excessive mucus. This substance also stimulates the production of digestive enzymes, which facilitates the breakdown of food and prevents the formation of mucus caused by food particles' putrefaction. Additionally, anise tea has antispasmodic effects, which soothe the gastrointestinal tract, reducing inflammation, and alleviating symptoms like bloating, cramps, and mucus production. In traditional medicine, anise tea has been used as a natural remedy for treating respiratory and gastrointestinal issues, such as asthma, bronchitis, and irritable bowel syndrome, due to its mucolytic and anti-inflammatory properties. Overall, anise tea offers a holistic approach to managing mucus-related conditions in the stomach, supporting digestion, and promoting overall gut health.
remedy_parts: Seeds: Anise seeds are commonly used in medicinal tea due to their volatile oil content, particularly anethole, which helps to reduce inflammation and mucus in the stomach.
Leaves: Anise leaves have been used in tea to soothe stomach ailments, as they contain flavonoids and other compounds that may help reduce mucus production.

Flowers: Anise flowers are less commonly used, but they can be included in tea blends for their mild taste and potential mucolytic properties, which may help break down and expel mucus from the stomach.
remedy_constituents: Anethole: Anethole, the main active constituent in anise, has expectorant properties that help loosen mucus in the stomach and promote its expulsion.
Fenchone: Fenchone, a monoterpene found in anise, has been shown to have anti-inflammatory effects, which may help reduce irritation and production of mucus in the stomach.
Limonene: Limonene, a compound also present in anise, has been found to have mucolytic properties, which means it can break down and thin mucus, making it easier to expel.
Anisic Alcohol: Anisic alcohol, a phenolic ether found in anise, may help soothe irritated mucous membranes in the stomach, reducing the production of mucus.

Methyl Chavicol: Methyl chavicol, also known as estragole, has been found to have antispasmodic and carminative properties, which can help reduce the production of gas in the stomach and soothe mucous membranes.
remedy_recipe: In a mortar, crush 1 teaspoon of anise seeds using a pestle to release their flavor.
Bring 1 cup of water to a boil in a small pot.
Add the crushed anise seeds to the boiling water, then reduce heat and simmer for 5 minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the solid remains of the seeds.
Drink the anise tea whilst warm, up to three times a day, to help soothe mucus in the stomach.

remedies.remedy_desc: Fennel tea helps with mucus in the stomach because it has antispasmodic and expectorant properties, which assist in breaking down and expelling mucus from the stomach. The tea's active component, anethole, relaxes the gastrointestinal tract, reducing inflammation and congestion in the stomach. Fennel tea's soothing warmth also encourages the expulsion of mucus, providing quick relief from discomfort. Furthermore, fennel tea has volatile oils that stimulate the production of gastric juices, aiding in digestion and reducing mucus buildup in the stomach. Lastly, fennel tea's antimicrobial properties help fight off any potential infections that may cause or worsen mucus production in the stomach.
remedy_parts: Seeds: Fennel seeds are rich in oil compounds that can help reduce inflammation and mucus production in the stomach.
Leaves: The leaves of fennel contain compounds that can soothe irritation in the digestive tract and help break down mucus.
Stems: Fennel stems have anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate symptoms of stomach mucus.
Flowers: Fennel flowers contain antioxidants that can support overall digestive health and reduce mucus production.
Rhizomes: The rhizomes of fennel can help stimulate the production of digestive enzymes and reduce mucus in the stomach.
remedy_constituents: Anethole: Anethole, the primary component of fennel's volatile oil, has expectorant and mucolytic properties, helping to break down and expel mucus from the stomach.

Fenchone: Fenchone, another component of fennel's essential oil, can help reduce inflammation in the stomach, alleviating symptoms of mucus associated with gastritis and other inflammatory conditions.
Essential Oil: Fennel's essential oil has antimicrobial properties, helping to eliminate harmful bacteria that can contribute to the production of mucus in the stomach.
Dianethole & Photoanethole: These compounds, found in smaller quantities in fennel, have been shown to have anti-inflammatory effects, which can help reduce the production of mucus in the stomach.
Flavonoids: Fennel contains flavonoids, which have antioxidant properties that can help protect the stomach lining from damage, reducing the formation of mucus.
Fiber: Fennel is a good source of dietary fiber, which can help regulate digestion and support overall stomach health, reducing the likelihood of mucus buildup.
remedy_recipe: Chop: Cut the stems and fronds off a fresh fennel bulb, setting aside a few fronds for garnish.
Measure: Boil 2 cups of water and add 1 tablespoon of roughly chopped fennel seeds.
Steep: Allow the fennel seeds to steep in the boiling water, covered, for 10-15 minutes.
Strain: Strain the tea to remove the fennel seeds and discard them.
Serve: Pour the fennel tea into a cup, garnish with reserved fennel fronds, and enjoy!

remedies.remedy_desc: Ginger tea helps with mucus in the stomach because it contains various bioactive compounds, such as gingerols and shogaols, which can reduce inflammation and irritation in the gastrointestinal tract. These compounds can help break down and expel gas in the digestive system, alleviating symptoms of mucus and congestion. Ginger tea is also known to stimulate the production of digestive enzymes, improving overall gut health and helping to eliminate excess mucus. Additionally, ginger has natural antibacterial properties, which can help fight off infections that may contribute to mucus buildup in the stomach. Lastly, ginger tea can help soothe the stomach lining and provide relief from discomfort, making it an effective natural remedy for reducing mucus in the stomach.
remedy_parts: Rhizomes: The underground stem of ginger, known as rhizomes, is commonly used to make medicinal tea for mucus in the stomach due to its anti-inflammatory and digestive properties.
Leaves: Ginger leaves have been used in traditional medicine to treat various ailments, including mucus in the stomach, due to their high antioxidant and antimicrobial properties.
Flowers: Ginger flowers are used to make medicinal tea to soothe mucus in the stomach because of their natural anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties.
Seeds: Ginger seeds are known for their potent bioactive compounds, making them a popular addition to medicinal tea for mucus in the stomach, helping to alleviate digestive issues.
remedy_constituents: Gingerols: Gingerols, the most abundant compound in ginger, have been shown to help reduce inflammation and mucus production in the stomach, providing relief for individuals with excess mucus.
Shogaols: Shogaols, a pungent compound formed when ginger is dried or cooked, exhibit anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate mucus buildup in the stomach.

Paradols: Paradols, derived from gingerols, have been found to inhibit the production of nitric oxide, a molecule involved in inflammation and mucus secretion, offering potential benefits for stomach mucus.
Gingerdiols: Gingerdiols demonstrate anti-inflammatory and mucus-reducing effects, contributing to ginger's overall ability to soothe the stomach and minimize mucus accumulation.
remedy_recipe: Peel and finely chop 1 inch of fresh ginger; place in a teapot or heat-resistant container.
Boil 4 cups of water, then pour over the chopped ginger; let steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the ginger pieces from the tea, leaving only the liquid.
Stir in 1-2 teaspoons of honey and a squeeze of lemon juice, if desired, for added flavor and soothing properties.
Serve the ginger tea warm, sipping slowly to alleviate mucus in the stomach.

remedies.remedy_desc: Chamomile tea helps with mucus in the stomach because it has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the lining of the stomach, which can lead to a decrease in mucus production. The tea's calming effects can also help relax the stomach muscles, thereby alleviating discomfort and pain caused by excess mucus. Chamomile tea is known for its mucolytic properties, which means it can break down and expel excess mucus, making it easier for the body to eliminate. Additionally, chamomile tea contains antispasmodic compounds that can help regulate muscle contractions, which can help prevent the production of excessive mucus in the stomach. Lastly, chamomile tea's soothing and gentle nature makes it a safe and effective natural remedy for treating mucus buildup in the stomach, especially for those who may be sensitive to over-the-counter medications.
remedy_parts: Flowers: The chamomile flowers are used in medicinal tea for mucus in the stomach due to their anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can help soothe digestive discomfort.
Leaves: Chamomile leaves are often included in the tea blend for their calming effects, which can help alleviate stress-related digestive issues.

Seeds: The seeds are used less frequently but can add extra therapeutic benefits, as they contain essential oils with carminative properties, helping to expel gas from the stomach and relieve bloating.
remedy_constituents: Chamazulene: This compound has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce irritation in the stomach and minimize the production of mucus.
Bisabolol: Known for its soothing effects, bisabolol can help alleviate stomach discomfort and decrease mucus buildup.
Matricin: This compound is an antiphlogistic, meaning it reduces inflammation, which can help control mucus secretion in the stomach.
Apigenin: A flavonoid with antispasmodic properties, apigenin can help relax stomach muscles, thereby reducing mucus production.
Luteolin: With both anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, luteolin can help protect the stomach lining and reduce mucus formation.
Quercetin: As an antioxidant and anti-inflammatory compound, quercetin can help alleviate mucus buildup by reducing inflammation in the stomach.
remedy_recipe: Boil 8 ounces of water in a kettle or saucepan, then let it cool down for a couple of minutes until it reaches 205-212°F (96-100°C).
Add 1-2 teaspoons of dried chamomile flowers or 1 chamomile tea bag to a teapot or mug.
Pour the hot water over the chamomile flowers or tea bag.
Cover and let it steep for 5-10 minutes for optimal flavor and health benefits.
Remove the chamomile flowers or tea bag, and enjoy your soothing chamomile tea. For added relief, consider adding a small amount of honey or lemon.

remedies.remedy_desc: Peppermint tea helps with mucus in the stomach because it contains menthol, a compound with natural antispasmodic properties that can help relax the gastrointestinal muscles and reduce inflammation. This action can alleviate the production of excess mucus in the stomach, which can lead to discomfort and irritation. Moreover, peppermint tea has been shown to increase the production of bile in the stomach, which can aid in the breakdown and digestion of food, further reducing the likelihood of mucus buildup. Additionally, peppermint tea has antibacterial and antiviral properties that can help fight off infections that may contribute to the overproduction of mucus in the stomach. However, it is important to note that while peppermint tea can be beneficial for some people with mucus in the stomach, it may exacerbate symptoms in others, particularly those with gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) or a hiatal hernia. As always, it is best to consult with a healthcare provider before incorporating new remedies into your routine.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the peppermint plant are commonly used in medicinal teas for mucus in the stomach due to their antispasmodic properties, which can help relax the gastrointestinal muscles and alleviate discomfort.
Stems: The stems of the peppermint plant contain menthol, which can help soothe inflammation in the stomach and thin mucus, making it easier to expel.

Flowers: Peppermint flowers contain essential oils that have been shown to have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects, making them useful for reducing inflammation in the stomach and relieving symptoms of mucus.
Seeds: Peppermint seeds are rich in antioxidants, which can help protect the stomach lining from damage and reduce the production of mucus.
remedy_constituents: Menthol: Peppermint's main active ingredient, menthol, has an anti-spasmodic effect which helps relax the gastrointestinal tract, reducing mucus and irritation in the stomach.
Methyl salicylate: With natural analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties, this compound in peppermint can help relieve pain and inflammation in the stomach, reducing mucus production.
Limonene: As a powerful antioxidant, limonene helps reduce damage from free radicals, which may contribute to mucus formation in the stomach.
Eugenol: This antiseptic and analgesic compound found in peppermint can help prevent the growth of harmful bacteria causing irritation and mucus build-up in the stomach.
remedy_recipe: In a tea infuser or teapot, place one peppermint tea bag or 1 tablespoon of loose peppermint leaves.
Pour boiling water (212°F) over the tea, enough to submerge the tea completely.
Let the tea steep for 5-10 minutes to allow the active compounds to fully dissolve and infuse the water.
Remove the tea bag or strain the loose leaves from the tea.
Enjoy the warm peppermint tea, up to three cups a day, for soothing relief of mucus in the stomach.

remedies.remedy_desc: Catnip tea helps with mucus in the stomach because it has natural antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the gastrointestinal tract, reducing irritation and inflammation. Additionally, catnip contains nepetalactone, a compound known to stimulate the release of digestive enzymes, which aids in breaking down food and eliminating excess mucus in the stomach. Lastly, drinking warm catnip tea can provide relief from congestion in the chest and throat, as the steam helps to open up the airways and loosen up mucus, making it easier to expel.

Flowers: Contain oils and acids that may help reduce inflammation and relieve symptoms of mucus in the stomach.
Stems: May have therapeutic benefits due to their tannin content, which can help alleviate mucus and soothe the stomach lining.

remedy_constituents: Nepetalactone: This compound is known for its anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce irritation and swelling in the stomach lining, thereby alleviating mucus production.

Citronellol: This monoterpenoid has been found to have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce mucus buildup caused by bacterial infections or inflammation in the stomach.
remedy_recipe: Boil 8 ounces of water and add 1 teaspoon of dried catnip leaves, letting it steep for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the leaves, discarding them afterwards.
Sip the tea slowly throughout the day, up to three times, to soothe a mucus-filled stomach.
